Description: Separates UI from business logic using ViewModel as intermediary. View observes ViewModel state changes reactively
When To Use: When building reactive UIs; when you need testable presentation logic; Flutter/SwiftUI/Compose projects
When Not To Use: Simple one-screen apps with minimal logic
Anti-Pattern: Putting business logic directly in the Widget/View build method

Description: Manages state through streams of events and states. Events go in states come out
When To Use: Flutter apps with complex state; when you need clear event-driven state transitions
When Not To Use: Simple UI with minimal state; when Provider/Riverpod suffice
Anti-Pattern: Managing state with setState() in complex screens

Description: Separates app into layers: Presentation Domain and Data. Dependencies point inward toward domain
When To Use: Large apps requiring testability; teams with multiple developers; long-lived projects
When Not To Use: Small prototypes; hackathon projects; simple CRUD apps
Anti-Pattern: Mixing data access code directly in UI widgets/views

Description: Abstracts data access behind an interface. Handles switching between remote API and local cache
When To Use: When you need offline-first; when data comes from multiple sources (API + DB + cache)
When Not To Use: When data comes from a single simple source
Anti-Pattern: Calling APIs directly from ViewModel/BLoC without abstraction

Description: View is passive and delegates all logic to Presenter. Presenter updates View through interface
When To Use: Legacy Android apps with XML layouts; when you need strict separation for testing
When Not To Use: When using declarative UI frameworks (Compose/SwiftUI/Flutter)
Anti-Pattern: Activity/Fragment implementing all business logic directly

Description: Unidirectional data flow: Intent -> Model -> View. Single immutable state represents entire screen
When To Use: Complex screens with many interactions; when debugging state issues; Jetpack Compose apps
When Not To Use: Simple forms; screens with minimal state
Anti-Pattern: Multiple mutable state variables that can get out of sync

Description: Centralizes navigation logic outside of screens. Each flow has its own coordinator
When To Use: Multi-step flows (onboarding checkout); deep linking support; large apps with complex navigation
When Not To Use: Single-screen apps; simple tab-based navigation
Anti-Pattern: Navigator.push/pop scattered throughout widgets with hardcoded routes

Description: Provides dependencies and reactive state throughout the widget tree using compile-safe providers
When To Use: Flutter apps needing DI and reactive state; when BLoC is too verbose
When Not To Use: When simple StatefulWidget state suffices
Anti-Pattern: Using global variables or singletons for state management

Description: Components subscribe to data streams and react to changes automatically
When To Use: For real-time data updates; when multiple UI components depend on same data source
When Not To Use: When data is fetched once and never changes
Anti-Pattern: Manually polling for updates or passing callbacks through multiple layers

Description: Converts between API/DB models and domain models to decouple layers
When To Use: When API response shape differs from domain needs; when switching API providers
When Not To Use: When models are identical across layers
Anti-Pattern: Using API response models directly in UI code

Description: Creates objects/widgets without specifying exact types. Useful for platform-specific implementations
When To Use: When creating platform-adaptive widgets; when object creation logic is complex
When Not To Use: When a simple constructor call suffices
Anti-Pattern: Long if/else chains checking platform in every widget

Description: Registers and resolves dependencies globally. Single instance of services throughout app lifecycle
When To Use: For core services (API client analytics logger); when full DI framework is overkill
When Not To Use: When dependency injection through constructors is feasible; in testable code
Anti-Pattern: Creating new instances of services everywhere instead of reusing

Description: Define a family of algorithms and swap them at runtime based on conditions
When To Use: For swappable auth methods (biometric PIN social); for different payment providers
When Not To Use: When there is only one implementation that will never change
Anti-Pattern: Hardcoding auth logic with if/else for different methods in one function

Description: Wraps existing functionality to add behavior like caching logging or retry without modifying original
When To Use: For adding caching/retry/logging around API calls or repositories
When Not To Use: When the original class can simply be modified
Anti-Pattern: Mixing caching logic inside API call methods

Description: Encapsulates operations as objects enabling undo/redo queuing and logging
When To Use: For text editors with undo; for queuing offline operations; for complex form workflows
When Not To Use: Simple one-shot operations that don't need undo
Anti-Pattern: Implementing undo by storing full state snapshots instead of operations

Description: Wraps HTTP requests/responses in layers for auth token refresh logging error handling
When To Use: For adding auth headers; for token refresh logic; for request/response logging
When Not To Use: When a simple one-off HTTP call with no cross-cutting concerns
Anti-Pattern: Adding auth headers manually in every API call

Description: Immutable data classes with union types pattern matching and copyWith for safe state management
When To Use: For all state objects; for API response models; for domain entities
When Not To Use: For simple throwaway data structures
Anti-Pattern: Using mutable classes for state causing accidental mutations

Description: Builds complex UIs by composing smaller reusable widgets following a design system
When To Use: For building consistent UI across the app; for design system implementation
When Not To Use: When a widget is truly one-off and will never be reused
Anti-Pattern: Copy-pasting styling code across multiple screens

Description: Single-responsibility classes encapsulating one business operation in the domain layer
When To Use: In Clean Architecture projects; when business logic needs to be tested independently
When Not To Use: Simple CRUD with no business rules
Anti-Pattern: Putting multi-step business logic directly in ViewModel/BLoC

Description: Strict five-layer separation: View Interactor Presenter Entity Router. Common in iOS native
When To Use: Large iOS teams needing strict separation; enterprise iOS apps
When Not To Use: Small apps; when SwiftUI with MVVM suffices; cross-platform projects
Anti-Pattern: Massive View Controllers with thousands of lines of mixed responsibilities

Description: Single global store with reducers handling actions. Predictable state with time-travel debugging
When To Use: Large apps needing predictable state; when debugging complex state transitions
When Not To Use: Small apps; when simpler state management suffices
Anti-Pattern: Multiple scattered state sources that can get out of sync

Description: Constructs complex objects step-by-step using a fluent interface. Common for request/config building
When To Use: For complex object construction with many optional parameters; for building queries/requests
When Not To Use: When a simple constructor with named parameters suffices
Anti-Pattern: Constructors with 10+ positional parameters

Description: Provides dependencies through constructors or containers rather than creating them internally
When To Use: Always in production code; for testable architecture; for swapping implementations
When Not To Use: Quick prototypes where testability is not a concern
Anti-Pattern: Creating dependencies inside classes with new/direct instantiation

Description: Decoupled communication between app components through a shared event channel
When To Use: For cross-module communication; for analytics events; for showing global messages (snackbars)
When Not To Use: When direct dependency injection of callbacks is cleaner; when events create spaghetti flow
Anti-Pattern: Passing callbacks through 5+ layers of widget tree

Description: Loads data in pages as user scrolls. Supports offset-based and cursor-based pagination
When To Use: For any list/feed with potentially large datasets; for API endpoints returning paginated data
When Not To Use: When the full dataset is small enough to load at once
Anti-Pattern: Loading all data at once and filtering in-memory on mobile
